clean_story: Empty time.  Mind’s restless hunger. "Boredom" comes from the French ennui—a heavy, dragging feeling, like a clock’s hands stuck in syrup. It’s not just "nothing to do. "It’s the gap between what your brain expects and what it gets. If boredom can rightly be perceived as a signal—it’s the body’s "low battery" warning for attention. Like a phone vibrating when unplugged too long. Studies show rats in featureless cages gnaw their own tails. Humans scroll endlessly, click bait, or invent games from nothing—like prisoners etching chessboards into walls. In 2014, researchers left people alone in a room with a button that shocked them. 67% of men and 25% of women pressed it—just to feel something. Boredom isn’t laziness. It’s the friction between a mind built for hunting, creating, or solving—and a world offering neither challenge nor reward. Three related subjects are attention spans, daydream mechanics, and sensory deprivation.
